title: Dog Swallowed Ibuprofen: Emergency Triage Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: If your dog ate ibuprofen, this is a medical emergency. Learn why Advil and Motrin are toxic to dogs and what you must do immediately to help.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ibuprofen ingestion is a life-threatening emergency for dogs that requires immediate veterinary intervention, as even small doses can cause fatal organ damage before symptoms appear.

What is ibuprofen and why is it dangerous for my dog?

Ibuprofen is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) commonly found in household brands like Advil and Motrin. While safe for humans to treat pain and fever, it is extremely toxic to dogs. A dog's body cannot break down this medication efficiently, leading to rapid accumulation in the bloodstream.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my dog ate ibuprofen?

  • Urgency Level: High. Ibuprofen ingestion is a life-threatening veterinary emergency.
  • Even a small dose can cause severe gastric ulcers, acute kidney failure, and neurological issues such as seizures or coma.
  • Internal damage often begins before the dog shows outward signs of distress; do not wait for symptoms to appear.

How does providing a photo of the packaging help the veterinarian?

  • A photo allows the medical team to see the exact strength (milligrams per pill) to calculate toxicity.
  • Vets can check the full ingredient list for other dangerous additives, such as xylitol, found in certain coated or liquid versions.
  • This information helps the team accurately assess the risk based on your dog's weight and provide faster treatment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ibuprofen has a narrow margin of safety in dogs; toxic effects commonly reported after ingestion include gastrointestinal irritation or ulceration, gastrointestinal hemorrhage, and renal damage. Neurologic signs, hypotension, ataxia, and cardiac effects may also be observed. An acute single ingestion of 100–125 mg/kg can result in vomiting, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, and anorexia, while renal failure may follow dosages of 175–300 mg/kg. Treatment for ibuprofen toxicosis consists of early decontamination through induced emesis (if recent exposure) followed by activated charcoal administration, gastrointestinal and renal protection, and supportive care. Repeated doses of activated charcoal every 6-8 hours may prevent reabsorption via enterohepatic recirculation. H1-receptor antagonists (ranitidine, famotidine, cimetidine) may aid in treating gastrointestinal ulcers.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3028)

faqs.question: How much ibuprofen is toxic to a dog?
faqs.answer: There is no safe dose of ibuprofen for dogs. Depending on the size of your pet, even a single 200mg pill can cause serious stomach ulceration or kidney damage.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of ibuprofen poisoning in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include vomiting (sometimes with blood), dark or tarry stools, loss of appetite, lethargy, increased thirst, and stumbling.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian. Doing so incorrectly can lead to aspiration pneumonia or other complications.
